parsed.reasoning: The main bug is aliasing: create_config() assigns config to DEFAULTS directly, so updates mutate the shared defaults dictionary and leak state across calls. I fixed this by creating a shallow copy bef
The configuration factory mutates and returns the shared DEFAULTS dictionary instead of creating an independent copy, causing aliasing and unintended global state changes.

The stale-data bug comes from create_config() returning the DEFAULTS dictionary by reference and from app.py caching a mutable config object. Any runtime update mutates shared state, so later reads ca
get_settings() returns stale or mutated config because create_config() aliases the shared DEFAULTS dict instead of creating a fresh copy, so changes persist across reads and overrides.

The bug is caused by shared mutable state: create_config() returns the DEFAULTS dict directly, and ConfigMiddleware caches that same object in _base, then apply_config() mutates it per request. As a r
A shared mutable config object is cached and returned by reference, so per-request overrides mutate state that leaks into later requests.

The profile update logic violated the invariant that display_name must always match name. I refactored the update function to centralize field handling and ensure that whenever name changes, display_n
The update logic fails to keep display_name synchronized when the name field changes, violating the profile invariant.

The bug is in update_profile: when first_name changes, full_name is not recomputed, so the derived field can become stale. The fix is to update full_name whenever either first_name or last_name change
The profile updater recomputes full_name only when last_name changes, so first_name updates leave full_name stale and out of sync.

The profile update logic fails to clear the cached greeting and reset verification when the email changes, so stale greeting state persists after an email update.
The bug is a stale cache issue: update_product() mutates the database but leaves the cached copy unchanged, so get_product() can return outdated data after an update. I fixed this by invalidating the
The cache is never invalidated after product updates, so get_product can return stale data instead of the current database state.

The bug is in catalog.py: update_product mutates the database entry but leaves any cached copy intact, so later get_product calls can return stale data from the cache. The fix is to invalidate the cac
The catalog updates the database but never invalidates the cached product, so subsequent reads can return stale price data from cache.

The stale data issue is caused by incomplete cache invalidation in catalog.update_product(). After a product update, the shared cache is invalidated but the local cache is left untouched. Since api.ge
Product updates invalidate the shared cache but leave the local cache populated, so API reads stale data from the local layer before checking the database.

The bug is that _default_host is computed once at import time from _settings, so later calls to configure() or reset_settings() do not affect get_host(). To make the settings behavior consistent and c
The host value is cached in the module-level _default_host at import time, so later configuration changes are not reflected by get_host().

The bug is caused by client.py capturing the config once at import time in _client_config. After reset_config() clears the cached config, the client still reads the stale dictionary reference, so it n
The client eagerly caches the config at import time, so reset_config() only clears the module config and does not refresh the client's stale _client_config.

The bug is caused by client.py capturing the config dictionary at import time. When reset_config() clears the module-level cache in config.py, subsequent config loads create a fresh dict, but client.p
The client captures a config dict at import time, so after reset_config() swaps in a fresh config object the client and handler keep reading stale values from the old reference.

parsed.reasoning: The bug is in process_batch: it accumulates all item values first and then calls snapshot() only once, which means the snapshot history does not reflect per-item processing. I refactored the function
The batch processor updates the counter for all items first and records only one snapshot at the end, so per-item snapshot history is never captured.

The processor emits only one event after the loop instead of emitting one event per item, so event counts cannot match items processed.

`process_batch` performs a single audit entry after the loop using the last item only, so the audit log records fewer entries than processed items.
The bug is caused by transform() reusing the module-level _last_result when the input is falsy, which means empty input incorrectly returns a stale previous result. The refactor removes that stateful
The function returns a module-level cached list even when the input is empty, so falsy inputs reuse stale state instead of producing a fresh empty list.

The crash/incorrect behavior comes from loader.load() leaving the previous status and data in place when source is empty or None. Because pipeline.run_pipeline() reads the loader state after each call
The loader never resets status to "empty" on empty or None input, so the pipeline can report a stale status from a previous run.

The pipeline reuses the previous call’s cached _last_best value when no current record matches, causing stale non-None results instead of returning None.

The retry_send function is incorrect because it loops over all retry attempts even when send(msg) succeeds, causing duplicate sends. Since send() always returns True in this codebase, retry_send shoul
The retry loop ignores successful sends because it continues only on failure and never stops early, so the function is harder to understand and does unnecessary extra sends.
The duplicate messages come from the retry loop continuing even after a successful send. Because send() appends the message to the store, any extra loop iterations after success can append again. The
The retry loop continues after a successful send, causing the same message to be appended and notified multiple times.
The duplicate messages are caused by the pipeline-level retry loop in pipeline.py. ingest() calls send_with_retry(), which already handles transient retries, but then the pipeline loops again without
The pipeline retries the sender without stopping after the first successful send, causing duplicate message appends and notifications.

The bug is that inventory is reserved before charging the wallet, but if charging fails, the code re-raises the exception without releasing the reservation. This leaves stock permanently reserved afte
The order flow fails to release reserved inventory when wallet charging raises an exception, leaving stock incorrectly held after a failed payment.

The bug is in order_service.place_order: it reserves inventory before payment, but if payment fails it re-raises the exception without releasing the reservation. That leaves stock stuck in the reserve
Payment failures re-raise an exception without releasing the previously reserved inventory, leaving stock stuck in the reserved state.

The bug is in order_service.place_order: it reserves inventory and writes an audit entry before payment, but on payment failure it re-raises without undoing either side effect. That leaves inventory p
Payment failures re-raise without compensating rollback, leaving inventory reserved and audit entries persisted after a failed order.

The pipeline computes raw statistics from normalized data instead of the original input, so the reported raw metrics are incorrect.

The access-control map omits the moderator role, so requests for its permissions incorrectly fall back to an empty set.

Guest access is defined in roles.py but omitted from the role dispatch table in auth.py, so guest requests fall back to the default no-access handler.

Service accounts are authenticated by middleware but denied by auth.py because authorize() lacks a service_account branch and falls back to the unknown-role deny path.

The limiter uses the wrong comparison operator, so it blocks only when count is greater than limit instead of when the limit has been reached.

The policy incorrectly uses logical OR instead of AND, so a request is allowed whenever either the rate check or quota check passes, rather than requiring both.

A premature return in the zero-amount branch skips appending the required audit ledger entry, so skipped payments are not recorded.

Cached charge requests return before logging, so repeated calls bypass the audit trail and create missing audit entries.

When inserting at a specific position, labels and values are updated asymmetrically because the label is inserted but the value is always appended, causing index misalignment.

Deleting a column only updates the headers list, leaving each row with the old column values, which causes header-row misalignment.

Feature lookups fail because callers may use camelCase names while the flags dictionary stores snake_case keys, so the lookup convention is inconsistent.
